title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a woman called Aisha talking about an urban beekeeping project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a woman called Aisha talking about an urban beekeeping project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A woman called Aisha talks about an urban beekeeping project.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Aisha and I help run City Hive, a project which looks after bees in the middle of our busy town. People often suppose bees need fields far from buildings, but parks, gardens and window boxes can provide plenty of flowers. Our first hives were installed not in a garden but on the roof of a [9]car park[/9], because it was flat, secure and close to an area planted with wildflowers. Since then, other organisations have invited us to place hives on suitable roofs around town.||The project is about much more than collecting honey. Our main purpose is to understand how healthy bee populations are in different city locations. Volunteers are welcome, but nobody approaches hives alone. During the introduction, participants are shown the clothing and equipment needed, and they learn how to recognise calm or worried [10]bee behaviour[/10]. This is more useful at the beginning than memorising different flowers. Every person visiting a hive is supplied with a protective suit, but volunteers must bring their own [11]closed shoes[/11], since sandals or open shoes are unsuitable near bees.||New volunteers usually expect to open hives and see honey immediately. This is done only with experienced supervision. Their first regular task is generally [12]plant counting[/12] in nearby green spaces, recording which flowers are available for bees at different points in the year. It helps us decide where more flowers are needed. We have recently worked with the council to create a new [13]wildflower strip[/13] beside a cycling path. People may think it is simply decorative, but it should provide food for insects throughout the summer. Signs will explain its purpose to passers-by.||Honey produced by the bees is collected in small amounts only when the hives have enough for their own needs. Last year we sold tiny jars at the market, but our most successful product was [14]beeswax candles[/14], made in volunteer workshops using wax removed during normal hive care. We also run learning events. Our school sessions are popular, and children particularly enjoy looking at pollen through a microscope. This year's public session that filled fastest was on [15]balcony planting[/15], showing residents how even a small outdoor area can be useful for bees.||Volunteers who complete our full spring and summer programme are invited to a [16]honey tasting[/16] evening, where they can compare honey from different city locations and discuss the results. We also give them a [17]project certificate[/17], recording practical activities completed. Finally, the roofs we currently use are not all easy for educational visits. We hope to build a ground-level [18]teaching garden[/18], with flowers, signs and a safely enclosed viewing hive, so more children and wheelchair users can take part. Our autumn fair will raise money for it. Applications for volunteering are now open online.

dialogue.speaker: aisha
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Aisha and I help run City Hive, a project which looks after bees in the middle of our busy town. People often suppose bees need fields far from buildings, but parks, gardens and window boxes can provide plenty of flowers. Our first hives were installed not in a garden but on the roof of a car park, because it was flat, secure and close to an area planted with wildflowers. Since then, other organisations have invited us to place hives on suitable roofs around town.||The project is about much more than collecting honey. Our main purpose is to understand how healthy bee populations are in different city locations. Volunteers are welcome, but nobody approaches hives alone. During the introduction, participants are shown the clothing and equipment needed, and they learn how to recognise calm or worried bee behaviour. This is more useful at the beginning than memorising different flowers. Every person visiting a hive is supplied with a protective suit, but volunteers must bring their own closed shoes, since sandals or open shoes are unsuitable near bees.||New volunteers usually expect to open hives and see honey immediately. This is done only with experienced supervision. Their first regular task is generally plant counting in nearby green spaces, recording which flowers are available for bees at different points in the year. It helps us decide where more flowers are needed. We have recently worked with the council to create a new wildflower strip beside a cycling path. People may think it is simply decorative, but it should provide food for insects throughout the summer. Signs will explain its purpose to passers-by.||Honey produced by the bees is collected in small amounts only when the hives have enough for their own needs. Last year we sold tiny jars at the market, but our most successful product was beeswax candles, made in volunteer workshops using wax removed during normal hive care. We also run learning events. Our school sessions are popular, and children particularly enjoy looking at pollen through a microscope. This year's public session that filled fastest was on balcony planting, showing residents how even a small outdoor area can be useful for bees.||Volunteers who complete our full spring and summer programme are invited to a honey tasting evening, where they can compare honey from different city locations and discuss the results. We also give them a project certificate, recording practical activities completed. Finally, the roofs we currently use are not all easy for educational visits. We hope to build a ground-level teaching garden, with flowers, signs and a safely enclosed viewing hive, so more children and wheelchair users can take part. Our autumn fair will raise money for it. Applications for volunteering are now open online.
